title: RxJS Basics
subtitle: Reactive programming with Observables — handle async streams elegantly
description: Master RxJS in Angular — Observables, operators, Subjects, error handling, and best practices for managing async data streams without memory leaks.
keyPoints: An Observable is a lazy stream of values over time — it does nothing until subscribed
Operators are pure functions that transform, filter, or combine streams without modifying the source
switchMap cancels the previous inner Observable when a new value arrives — essential for HTTP search
Subjects are both Observable and Observer — they multicast to multiple subscribers
BehaviorSubject holds the latest value and immediately emits it to new subscribers
Always unsubscribe or use takeUntilDestroyed() to prevent memory leaks in components
The async pipe in templates handles subscribe and unsubscribe automatically

sections.title: What Is RxJS? Observable, Observer, Subscription
sections.content:
RxJS (Reactive Extensions for JavaScript) is a library for composing asynchronous and event-based programs using Observable sequences. It treats everything as a stream — HTTP responses, user clicks, timer ticks, WebSocket messages — and gives you a consistent, composable API to transform and combine those streams.

Three core concepts:

1. Observable — a blueprint for a stream. It defines what values will be emitted and when. An Observable is LAZY — it does nothing until someone subscribes to it. Creating an Observable is like writing a function definition; it only runs when called.

2. Observer — an object with three callbacks: next(value) called for each emitted value, error(err) called if the stream errors (terminates the stream), complete() called when the stream finishes normally (terminates the stream).

3. Subscription — what you get when you subscribe. It represents the active execution of the Observable. Call .unsubscribe() to stop receiving values and clean up resources.

sections.caption: An Observable only runs when subscribed. It emits next() values until either error() or complete() terminates it. Subscription.unsubscribe() cancels early.

sections.title: Creating Observables — of(), from(), interval(), fromEvent()
sections.explanation: The $ suffix is a convention for Observable variables — it is not enforced by TypeScript but makes it obvious that a variable is a stream. from() is especially useful for converting Promises to Observables so you can use RxJS operators on them.

sections.title: Operators — Transforming Streams
sections.content:
Operators are the core of RxJS. They are pure functions that take an Observable and return a new Observable — the source is never modified. You chain operators with pipe().

Transformation operators:
• map(fn)            — transform each value: map(x => x * 2)
• pluck('key')       — extract a property from each object (deprecated — use map(o => o.key))
• scan(fn, seed)     — like Array.reduce but emits after every value
• switchMap(fn)      — maps to inner Observable, CANCELS previous if new value arrives
• mergeMap(fn)       — maps to inner Observable, runs ALL concurrently
• concatMap(fn)      — maps to inner Observable, queues them — waits for each to finish
• exhaustMap(fn)     — ignores new values while inner Observable is still running

Filtering operators:
• filter(predicate)  — only pass values that satisfy the predicate
• take(n)            — take the first n values then complete
• takeUntil(obs$)    — complete when a notifier Observable emits
• first() / last()   — take only the first/last value
• distinctUntilChanged() — skip duplicate consecutive values
• debounceTime(ms)   — wait for silence before emitting (search inputs)
• throttleTime(ms)   — emit at most once per interval

Utility operators:
• tap(fn)            — peek at values for side effects, passes them through unchanged
• delay(ms)          — delay emissions by ms
• finalize(fn)       — run a callback when stream completes or errors
• shareReplay(n)      — multicast + cache last n values for late subscribers

sections.title: Essential Operators — map, filter, tap, debounceTime
sections.explanation: tap() is your best debugging tool — add it anywhere in a pipe() chain to inspect values without changing the stream. distinctUntilChanged() + debounceTime(300) is the standard pattern for any search input.

sections.title: switchMap vs mergeMap vs concatMap vs exhaustMap

sections.caption: switchMap is almost always what you want for HTTP requests (cancel stale requests). concatMap for sequential operations. mergeMap for parallel (fire-and-forget). exhaustMap for preventing duplicate form submits.

sections.title: Higher-Order Operators — switchMap, concatMap, exhaustMap
sections.explanation: The choice of mapping operator completely changes the behaviour of your stream. switchMap is almost always correct for search / autocomplete. exhaustMap is the correct tool for form submit buttons. concatMap for anything that must happen in order.

sections.title: Subjects — Observable + Observer in One
sections.content:
A Subject is both an Observable and an Observer. This means you can subscribe to it like an Observable AND push values into it imperatively. It multicasts — every subscriber receives the same emission.

Four types of Subject:

1. Subject — plain multicast. New subscribers only get values emitted AFTER they subscribe. Think of it like a live radio station — tune in late and you miss what already played.

2. BehaviorSubject(initialValue) — stores the LATEST value. New subscribers immediately receive the current value. Think of it like a variable that also notifies listeners. Most common in Angular services for shared state.

3. ReplaySubject(bufferSize) — replays the last N values to new subscribers. Useful for caching recent events.

4. AsyncSubject — only emits the LAST value, and only when the source completes. Rarely used.

In Angular, Subjects power:
• Services that broadcast events to multiple components
• Triggering actions imperatively (click a button → push to Subject → Observable chain reacts)
• Cross-component communication

sections.title: Subject and BehaviorSubject
sections.explanation: Always expose Subjects as read-only Observables using .asObservable(). If you expose the Subject directly, any component could call .next() on it and corrupt your service state. The private Subject + public Observable pattern is the Angular equivalent of private/readonly fields.

sections.title: Error Handling — catchError, retry, retryWhen
sections.content:
Error handling in RxJS is declarative — you handle errors as part of the operator chain, not with try/catch.

catchError(fn) — intercept an error and either:
  • Return a fallback Observable: catchError(() => of(defaultValue))
  • Re-throw to propagate: catchError(err => throwError(() => err))
  • Return EMPTY to silently complete the stream

retry(n) — if the Observable errors, resubscribe up to n times before propagating the error.

retry({ count, delay }) — retry with delay between attempts (Angular 14+). Great for transient network errors.

Important: when an Observable errors and you don't catch it, the entire stream terminates and your subscriber's error handler is called. The subscription is over — it will not receive future values. Always catch errors in long-lived streams (form valueChanges, interval, etc.).

sections.title: Error Handling in Streams
sections.explanation: Critical insight: catchError must be inside switchMap when dealing with long-lived streams. If HTTP errors and catchError is outside, the whole stream terminates. If catchError is inside the switchMap callback (on the inner Observable), only that one request fails — the search stream keeps working for future queries.

sections.title: Memory Leaks — takeUntilDestroyed and async Pipe
sections.content:
The most common RxJS bug in Angular is forgetting to unsubscribe. If you subscribe in ngOnInit and never unsubscribe, the Observable keeps running after the component is destroyed — causing memory leaks and 'cannot read property of undefined' errors on destroyed component views.

Three ways to manage subscriptions:

1. async pipe (best for templates) — the template subscribes and automatically unsubscribes when the component is destroyed. No code needed. Use this whenever displaying Observable data in a template.

2. takeUntilDestroyed() — a pipeable operator that completes the Observable when the component is destroyed. Call it anywhere in a pipe() chain. Works in Angular 16+.

3. Manual unsubscribe — store the Subscription and call .unsubscribe() in ngOnDestroy. More verbose but works everywhere.

toSignal() also auto-unsubscribes — if you convert an Observable to a Signal in a component, the subscription is cleaned up automatically on component destroy.

sections.title: Preventing Memory Leaks
sections.explanation: Prefer async pipe in templates — it is the simplest and safest pattern. Use takeUntilDestroyed() for subscriptions in class logic (constructor, methods). Use toSignal() when you want to use the data in a Signal-based component.

sections.title: Combination Operators — forkJoin, combineLatest, merge
sections.explanation: forkJoin is the correct tool for parallel loading at page init (load all data before rendering). combineLatest is the correct tool for reactive filtering/sorting UIs where any change in filters should trigger a new query.

sections.content: Use shareReplay(1) when the same HTTP Observable is subscribed to by multiple components (e.g. current user profile, app config). Without it, each subscriber triggers a separate HTTP request. With shareReplay(1), only one request is made and the response is replayed to all subscribers, including those that subscribe later.

Angular Signals and RxJS are complementary, not competing. Signals are better for synchronous state in components and services. RxJS is better for complex async workflows — debouncing, retries, cancellation, parallel requests, event streams. Use toSignal() and toObservable() to cross the boundary freely. Many real Angular apps use both.

interviewQuestions.q: What is an Observable and how is it different from a Promise?
interviewQuestions.a: An Observable is a lazy, cancellable stream of zero or more values over time. A Promise is eager, non-cancellable, and resolves exactly once. Key differences: (1) Lazy — an Observable does nothing until subscribed; a Promise starts executing immediately on creation. (2) Multiple values — an Observable can emit many values over time; a Promise settles once. (3) Cancellable — you can .unsubscribe() from an Observable; Promises cannot be cancelled. (4) Operators — Observables compose with map, filter, switchMap, etc.; Promises only have .then(). (5) Error recovery — catchError, retry operators provide powerful error handling; Promises only have .catch().

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap, and exhaustMap?
interviewQuestions.a: All four map each source value to an inner Observable, but differ in how they handle overlapping inner Observables. switchMap: cancels the previous inner Observable when a new value arrives — best for search (cancel stale HTTP requests). mergeMap: runs all inner Observables concurrently with no ordering guarantee — best for independent parallel operations. concatMap: queues inner Observables and waits for each to complete before starting the next — best for operations that must happen in order (sequential uploads). exhaustMap: ignores new source values while the current inner Observable is active — best for preventing duplicate form submissions (ignore extra clicks).

interviewQuestions.q: What is a Subject and how does it differ from a regular Observable?
interviewQuestions.a: A Subject is both an Observable and an Observer. Unlike a regular Observable (which is unicast — each subscriber gets its own execution), a Subject is multicast — all subscribers share the same execution and receive the same values. You can push values imperatively with .next(). A regular Observable's data source is defined in its constructor and cannot be pushed to from outside. Use a Subject when you need to bridge imperative code (button clicks, events) with reactive streams, or to multicast a value to multiple subscribers.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between Subject, BehaviorSubject, and ReplaySubject?
interviewQuestions.a: Subject: plain multicast — new subscribers only receive values emitted AFTER they subscribe. They miss all previous values. BehaviorSubject(initialValue): stores the most recent value. New subscribers immediately receive the current value. Use for state that components need to know right away (current user, theme, cart count). ReplaySubject(n): stores and replays the last n values to any new subscriber. Use for caching recent events (last N log entries, recent search history). AsyncSubject: only emits the last value when the source completes — rarely used.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you prevent memory leaks from RxJS subscriptions in Angular components?
interviewQuestions.a: Three main approaches: (1) async pipe in templates — the template automatically subscribes and unsubscribes when the component is destroyed. Zero code needed — this is the preferred approach for template data. (2) takeUntilDestroyed() — a pipeable operator (Angular 16+) that completes the Observable when the component is destroyed. Call it in a pipe() chain in the constructor or a field initialiser. (3) toSignal() — converts Observable to Signal and auto-unsubscribes. (4) Manual: store the Subscription in a property and call .unsubscribe() in ngOnDestroy. The most error-prone — avoid unless the others aren't suitable.

interviewQuestions.q: What does the catchError operator do and why must it be placed inside switchMap for long-lived streams?
interviewQuestions.a: catchError intercepts an error in a stream and returns a fallback Observable (preventing the error from propagating). The critical rule: when an Observable errors, the stream terminates — the subscriber's error handler is called and the subscription is over. In a long-lived stream like search (switchMap over valueChanges), if catchError is outside the switchMap, a single HTTP error terminates the entire search stream — no more search results ever. If catchError is inside the switchMap callback (on the inner Observable), only that one inner request fails gracefully. The outer stream (valueChanges) keeps running, so the next keystroke triggers a fresh search.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between forkJoin, combineLatest, and merge?
interviewQuestions.a: forkJoin: runs all Observables in parallel and emits ONE combined result when ALL of them complete. Like Promise.all(). Best for loading multiple independent resources at page init. Errors if any source errors. combineLatest: emits a combined array/object whenever ANY source emits, always using the latest value from all sources. Only starts emitting after all sources have emitted at least once. Best for reactive filter/sort UIs where any change in a dropdown should trigger a new query. merge: subscribes to all sources and re-emits values as they arrive, with no coordination. Best for merging independent event streams.

interviewQuestions.q: What is tap() and when would you use it?
interviewQuestions.a: tap() is a utility operator that lets you perform side effects for each value in a stream without modifying the stream. It passes every value through unchanged. Common uses: (1) Debugging — add tap(v => console.log(v)) anywhere in a pipe chain to inspect values at that point. (2) Updating loading state — tap({ next: () => loading.set(false), error: () => loading.set(false) }). (3) Logging analytics events. (4) Triggering notifications on success. The key property of tap() is that it does NOT alter the values or the stream's timing — it is purely observational.

interviewQuestions.q: What is shareReplay() and when is it necessary?
interviewQuestions.a: shareReplay(bufferSize) makes an Observable multicast (shared among all subscribers) and caches the last bufferSize emissions for late subscribers. Without it, every subscriber causes the Observable's factory function to re-execute — meaning every component that subscribes to http.get('/api/user') triggers a separate HTTP request. With shareReplay(1), only one HTTP request is made, the response is cached, and any component that subscribes later (even after the request completes) immediately receives the cached value. Use it for: shared HTTP calls (user profile, app config), expensive computations, and any Observable that multiple parts of the app need to subscribe to.

interviewQuestions.q: When would you choose RxJS over Angular Signals, and vice versa?
interviewQuestions.a: Choose Signals for: synchronous state in components and services, derived computed values, simple UI state (isOpen, selectedTab, count), replacing @Input()/@Output(), and avoiding Zone.js (zoneless apps). Choose RxJS for: complex async workflows with multiple steps (search: debounce → distinct → HTTP cancel → retry → error handling), event streams (mouse moves, keyboard, WebSocket), time-based operations (debounce, throttle, delay, buffer), combining multiple async sources (forkJoin, combineLatest), and anywhere cancellation is needed. In practice, use both: model state with Signals, express async data flows with RxJS, and bridge between them with toSignal() and toObservable().
